candidates.case_name: YINGGAO RESOURCES LTD AND ANOTHER v. THE HONGKONG AND SHANGHAI BANKING CORPORATION LTD
judges: Before: Master Alexander Tang in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The test for remoteness of damage in contract comprises two limbs (actual and imputed contemplation), which together express the principle that a party is only liable for losses that were, at the time of contracting, within their reasonable contemplation as a possible result of breach. Both limbs are assessed objectively.

principles.principle_label: Amendment of pleadings - bound to fail test
principles.paraphrase_en: An amendment to a pleading should only be disallowed at an early stage if the proposed claim is bound to fail. The court will not refuse an amendment merely because the losses claimed may not ultimately be recoverable as a matter of remoteness, if it cannot be concluded they must be regarded as not flowing naturally from the breach.

principles.principle_label: Cause of action - essential factual elements
principles.paraphrase_en: A 'cause of action' consists of every material fact that must be proved to entitle the plaintiff to succeed. To identify these facts, the litmus test is whether they must be pleaded to sustain the claim and avoid it being struck out. The pleadings must be reduced to their essential factual elements for analysis.

principles.principle_label: Loss as element in contract vs tort
principles.paraphrase_en: Loss is not an essential element of a cause of action for breach of contract, but it is an essential element of a cause of action in tort for breach of a duty of care.

Where loss is an essential element of a cause of action (as in tort), the essential element constituting the cause of action is that the plaintiff suffered some loss as a result of the pleaded breach, not that the plaintiff suffered a specific type or quantification of loss. Therefore, substituting or changing the pleaded losses may not necessarily introduce a new cause of action.

relationships.target_case_name: Jackson v Royal Bank of Scotland plc
relationships.description: The judgment cites Lord Walker's observation from this case regarding the two limbs of the remoteness test being the practical expression of a single principle.

relationships.target_case_name: Shenzhen Futaihong (CA)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the approach from this case that requires boiling pleadings down to their essential factual elements to determine the cause of action.

relationships.target_case_name: Berezovsky
relationships.description: The judgment follows the ratio from this case that the essential element in a tort claim is suffering loss from the breach, not suffering a specific loss.

relationships.target_case_name: Steamship Mutual Underwriting Association Ltd v Trollope & Colls (City) Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment notes counsel's reliance on May LJ's statement from this case regarding the test for new causes of action not being cut and dried.

principles.principle_label: Relevance of expert evidence at interlocutory stage
principles.paraphrase_en: At an interlocutory stage, the court should not preclude a party from adducing expert evidence merely because factual matters related to that evidence (such as reasons for non-compliance with treatment) are contested and will be determined at trial.
Psychiatric expert evidence can be relevant and of probative value to issues of causation and quantum in a case.

principles.principle_label: Granting leave to adduce expert evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may grant leave for a party to adduce expert evidence when it is deemed appropriate for the proper determination of the case.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Yin Chau
relationships.description: The present judgment cites and summarizes the ruling in Wong Yin Chau where the court allowed an appeal for leave to adduce psychiatric expert evidence.

candidates.case_name: B K AMRIT v. G-CLADDS LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Marlene Ng J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Appellate review of case management decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will not lightly interfere with a case management decision made by a master or judge, especially concerning expert evidence arrangements, unless there are good reasons to do so.

principles.principle_label: Oral expert evidence as case management decision
principles.paraphrase_en: The decision on whether to require oral expert evidence at trial is a pre-eminently case-sensitive case management matter, and limited reliance can be placed on other case authorities as each case depends on its specific context.

principles.principle_label: Test for allowing oral expert evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: The guiding principle for allowing oral expert evidence at trial is whether it is necessary in the interests of justice for the proper and just resolution of the case.

Causation in a personal injury case is a matter for the judge to determine using a common-sense approach and applying the civil standard of proof (balance of probabilities) to the medical evidence; it is not a matter for medical experts to decide, as they may apply a different standard.

relationships.target_case_name: Chau Chin To Chadow v Wing Fung Financial Group Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment refers to this case as containing relevant legal principles on expert evidence, which were noted by Master Roy Yu.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheung Oi Yan Ruby v Wong Hoi Sum
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Court of Appeal authority for the observation that resolving expert disagreements based on joint reports alone can be difficult, highlighting the potential need for oral evidence.

relationships.target_case_name: Homebase Limited v ATS Rengasamy
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example where oral expert evidence was deemed necessary because the experts disagreed on a key issue central to determining liability.

relationships.target_case_name: Lee Kin-kai, a patient by his father and next friend Li Wah v Ocean Tramping Co Ltd t/a Ocean Tramping Workshop
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that causation is for the judge to determine using a common-sense approach and the balance of probabilities standard.

relationships.target_case_name: Chan Shek Ho v Shiu Ho-chi & ors
relationships.description: The judge notes having held similar views on the legal approach to causation in this earlier case.

candidates.case_name: TAIPING TRUSTEES LTD v. BOSC INTERNATIONAL COMPANY LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of expert evidence on professional standards
principles.paraphrase_en: Expert evidence is necessary to assist the court in determining whether a professional party has discharged its duties, as the court will be hesitant to find a breach of duty without evidence on the standards within the relevant profession.

principles.principle_label: Appellate intervention in case management decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will only intervene in a case management decision from a lower court if the decision was based on an error of principle or was clearly wrong, representing a very high threshold for appeal.

principles.principle_label: De novo hearing for appeals from masters to judges
principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal from a case management decision of a master to a judge in chambers is treated as a de novo hearing, where the judge re-hears the application afresh.

principles.principle_label: Limitation on expert evidence for factual inferences
principles.paraphrase_en: Expert evidence should not be admitted if the question can be determined based on factual evidence and the inferences drawn from it, and where expert evidence would not be helpful.

principles.principle_label: Framing of expert questions to avoid ultimate issues
principles.paraphrase_en: Questions posed to experts should generally avoid asking them to answer the ultimate question to be decided by the court, unless it is necessary and appropriate in the specific circumstances of the case.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Kar Gee Mimi v Severn Villa Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles stated by Kwan JA regarding the high threshold for appealing case management decisions.

relationships.target_case_name: Peace Mark (Holdings) Ltd v Chau Cham Wong Patrick
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that expert questions should generally avoid answering the ultimate issue for the court.

relationships.target_case_name: Formosa Taffeta Co Ltd v Banque Indosuez
relationships.description: The judgment cites Stone J's comments on the framing of expert questions to avoid ultimate issues.

principles.principle_label: Elements of common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust requires three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding beneficial interests, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner departs from it.

principles.principle_label: Two situations for common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust can arise in two situations: first, where there is an express agreement, arrangement, or understanding between the parties about beneficial ownership, based on evidence of express discussions; second, where there is no such express agreement but the court infers a common intention from the parties' conduct.

principles.principle_label: Presumption of resulting trust from financial contribution
principles.paraphrase_en: A resulting trust arises where a person pays wholly or in part for property vested in another's or joint names, creating a presumption they did not intend a gift; the property is held on trust for them in shares proportionate to their contributions. This presumption is rebuttable.

principles.principle_label: Applicability of trusts to HOS flats
principles.paraphrase_en: In principle, Home Ownership Scheme flats can be subject to constructive or resulting trusts in favour of contributors to the purchase price, which serves the public interest by allowing low-income persons to benefit from contributions.

relationships.target_case_name: Primecredit Ltd v Yeung Chun Pang Barry
relationships.description: The judgment applies the two situations for common intention constructive trust identified in Primecredit.

relationships.target_case_name: Westdeutsche Landesbank Girozentrale

relationships.target_case_name: Cheuk Shu Yin v Yip Po Wan and another

principles.paraphrase_en: For a common intention constructive trust to arise, there must be a shared intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding beneficial ownership, and the claimant must have acted to their detriment in reliance on that common intention, making it unconscionable for the owner to deny the claimant's interest.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for trust claims
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a property is registered in the name of the defendant, the legal burden of proof rests on the plaintiff to establish, on the balance of probabilities, that the defendant holds the property on trust for the plaintiff.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for beneficial interest
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal title to property determines the beneficial interest unless proven otherwise. The party claiming a different beneficial arrangement bears the burden of proof.

A common intention constructive trust arises from a shared understanding between the property owner and claimant about beneficial interests, coupled with the claimant's detrimental reliance on that understanding, making it unconscionable for the owner to depart from it.

When a person voluntarily pays for property vested in another's name or joint names, the law presumes they did not intend a gift. The property is held on resulting trust for the payer, proportionate to their contribution, unless rebutted by evidence of a contrary intention.

principles.principle_label: Mortgage contribution timing for resulting trust
principles.paraphrase_en: For resulting trust purposes, mortgage funds used at purchase are considered a contribution by the person obligated to repay the loan at that time. Subsequent mortgage payments by someone not originally obligated do not count as a purchase contribution.

principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust requires three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding beneficial interests, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner departs from that intention.

A common intention agreement or arrangement for a constructive trust must be based on evidence of express discussions between the parties, even if imperfectly remembered or imprecise in terms. It typically arises prior to acquisition or, exceptionally, later.

When determining claims for a common intention constructive trust or resulting trust, the court should adopt a holistic approach rather than a piecemeal analysis of numerous factual issues, to avoid losing sight of the broader context.
The parties' initial intentions regarding beneficial ownership of property may change over time, such as where one party finances a substantial improvement that significantly alters the property.

Where a declaration is the primary relief sought, it may be necessary to grant it, but its scope must not exceed what the plaintiff is entitled to and what justice requires.

A constructive trust based on common intention requires three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding shared beneficial interests, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner departs from that intention.

A common intention constructive trust can arise in two situations: first, where there is an agreement, arrangement, or understanding between the parties about beneficial ownership reached before acquisition (or exceptionally later), based on evidence of express discussions; second, where the court infers or imputes a common intention from the parties' conduct.

principles.paraphrase_en: The legal owner of a property is presumed to also be the beneficial owner. A party claiming a beneficial interest contrary to the legal title bears the burden of proving that interest on the balance of probabilities.

principles.principle_label: Common intention constructive trust - fundamental principle
principles.paraphrase_en: The doctrine of common intention constructive trust is underpinned by the fundamental equitable principle of preventing unconscionable conduct. The court must assess all elements of the doctrine holistically.
For a common intention constructive trust, the relevant intention is that which is actually and mutually held by the parties. It is not sufficient for one party to have a unilateral intention.
In determining whether a common intention exists, the court considers express discussions or agreements between the parties, or direct contributions to the purchase price.
An imperfectly remembered or imprecise agreement between parties is not an automatic barrier to the court finding that an express agreement, arrangement, or understanding existed.

principles.paraphrase_en: For a constructive trust based on common intention to arise, there must be a shared intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding beneficial ownership, detrimental reliance by the claimant on that intention, and it would be unconscionable for the owner to deny that interest.

principles.paraphrase_en: When property is registered in another's name, the person claiming beneficial ownership bears the burden of proving, on the balance of probabilities, that the registered owner holds the property on trust for them.

principles.principle_label: Establishment of common intention constructive trust

principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust arises from three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and claimant regarding shared beneficial interests, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner departs from that intention.
Where property is transferred without payment of consideration, a resulting trust arises in favor of the transferor, meaning the transferee holds the property on trust for the transferor.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for beneficial interest
principles.paraphrase_en: When property is registered in a defendant's sole name, the legal presumption is that the beneficial interest follows the legal title. The plaintiff bears the burden of proving, on the balance of probabilities, that the defendant holds the property on trust for them, thereby separating beneficial from legal ownership.

principles.principle_label: Requirements for common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish a common intention constructive trust, a plaintiff must demonstrate: (1) a common intention between the plaintiff and property owner at the time of purchase (or later) that beneficial ownership differs from legal ownership; (2) the plaintiff altered their position in detrimental reliance on that common intention; and (3) it would be unconscionable for the property owner to rely solely on legal title.

principles.principle_label: Two limbs of common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: The doctrine of common intention constructive trust has two limbs: first, based on an express agreement, arrangement, or understanding between parties regarding beneficial ownership; second, where no express agreement exists, the court may infer a common intention from the parties' conduct.

principles.principle_label: Priority of common intention over resulting trust
principles.paraphrase_en: In domestic property disputes, if the matter can be resolved by reference to common intention constructive trust principles, it is unnecessary to resort to resulting trust analysis.

When a person makes a voluntary payment toward property vested in another's name, or pays for property in another's name, the law presumes they did not intend a gift. The property is held on resulting trust for the payer, proportionate to their contribution, unless rebutted by evidence of an intention to benefit the legal owner or by the presumption of advancement.

principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust can arise when parties reach an agreement, arrangement, or understanding about beneficial ownership before property acquisition (or exceptionally later).

principles.paraphrase_en: A constructive trust based on common intention requires three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding beneficial interests, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner departs from that intention.

A common intention agreement or arrangement for beneficial ownership must be based on evidence of express discussions between the parties, even if the terms are imperfectly remembered or imprecise. Such an agreement is typically found prior to acquisition, or exceptionally at a later date.

principles.principle_label: Change of intention through property improvement
principles.paraphrase_en: The parties' initial intentions regarding beneficial ownership may change if one party finances or constructs a substantial extension or improvement to the property, thereby significantly altering the asset from its original state.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for beneficial ownership claim
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant asserting sole beneficial ownership must establish, on the totality of the evidence including testimony, inherent probabilities, and documentary evidence, that they had the financial means to acquire the property and that there was a common intention inconsistent with the registered co-ownership.

principles.principle_label: Establishment of common intention constructive trust

principles.paraphrase_en: A Quistclose trust arises when money is transferred with the objective intention that it be used solely for a specific purpose, preventing the recipient from obtaining a beneficial interest in the funds while that purpose remains achievable.
The requisite intention for a Quistclose trust is not an intention to retain a beneficial interest, but the objective intention that the funds be applied only for a specific designated purpose.

An express undertaking by the recipient to use funds solely for a specific purpose is not necessary; a Quistclose trust can arise from circumstances that objectively justify inferring such an intention.

principles.principle_label: Nature of Quistclose trust (resulting trust analysis)
principles.paraphrase_en: A Quistclose trust is properly analyzed as a resulting trust for the transferor from the outset, with the transferee holding a mandate to apply the money for the stated purpose, meaning the lender never parts with the entire beneficial interest.

The central question in every case is whether the parties intended the money to be at the free disposition of the recipient; a trust arises if the money was to be used exclusively for a particular purpose, making the recipient a fiduciary in respect of it.

principles.principle_label: Resulting trust vs. common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: Where there is clear evidence of a common intention between parties regarding property ownership, and one party has acted to their detriment in reliance on that intention, the doctrine of resulting trust has no application. principles.principle_label: Whole course of conduct in determining shared intention
principles.paraphrase_en: In determining the parties' shared intention regarding beneficial ownership of a property, the court must consider their whole course of conduct in relation to the property. This includes looking at the history of their affairs concerning other jointly held assets and their distribution.

The court's search for a couple's shared intention regarding beneficial interests should not be confined to the specific property in question. Deciding factors can include the parties' respective earnings and borrowing power at acquisition, prevailing social policies (taxes, duties, interest rates), and the overall financial arrangements for all properties held.

principles.principle_label: Equivocal statements insufficient for sole ownership
principles.paraphrase_en: A statement by one party that they will make all mortgage repayments is, on its own, too equivocal to establish an intention for sole beneficial ownership. This is especially so when considered against the couple's usual financial arrangements and the property's use as a family home.

principles.principle_label: Presumption of beneficial interest following legal title

relationships.target_case_name: In re Collens

candidates.case_name: SUCCESS LANE DEVELOPMENT LTD v. IFC DEVELOPMENT (SUITE) LTD AS AGENT FOR IFC DEVELOPMENT LTD
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Alan Kwong in Chambers (Open to the Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A corporation may have standing to bring a claim for harassment in its own capacity, not because it can experience emotional distress, but because it may incur expenses to address harassment affecting its employees.
Courts will not decide hypothetical or academic questions that have no practical effect on the parties' rights or interests.

relationships.target_case_name: Sir Elly Kadoorie
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Court of Appeal's reasoning that a corporation may bring a harassment claim based on costs incurred to address employee harassment.

relationships.target_case_name: Koo Ming Kown
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that courts should not adjudicate academic controversies without practical utility.

candidates.case_name: L, T v. M, MD
judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Sharon D. Melloy in Chambers (Not open to public). Paper disposal

principles.paraphrase_en: Leave to appeal requires an arguable case with a reasonable chance of success. An appellate court will not interfere with the exercise of a lower court's discretion unless it was clearly incorrect, or the judge was wrong on the law or a legal principle.
A party's reprehensible conduct during proceedings is a relevant factor for the court to consider when determining liability for costs.
There is a general rule that a party in a position to pay their own costs should do so, and a court will not lightly order another party to pay them in full absent good reason.

candidates.case_name: WOO TAK YAN v. SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Alan Kwong in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will not interfere with a lower tribunal's factual findings if they were based on a careful consideration of the available evidence and the tribunal correctly identified the issues in dispute.

The test for apparent bias is whether a fair-minded and informed observer, having considered all relevant circumstances, would conclude there was a real possibility or real danger that the tribunal was biased.
A tribunal must apply the 'reasonable apprehension' test to determine if there is apparent bias, and adverse findings or comments alone do not satisfy this test.

relationships.target_case_name: GM-SA aka DG, M-S v DDPJ (Recusal)
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts and applies the established 'reasonable apprehension' test for apparent bias as articulated in that case.

candidates.case_name: HA CHE WAI v. MEDICAL SUPERINTENDENT OF THE PAMELA YOUDE NETHERSOLE EASTERN PSYCHIATRIC OBSERVATION UNIT AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

principles.principle_label: Common law supplementation of statutory procedures
principles.paraphrase_en: The common law will intervene to supplement statutory procedures to ensure fairness, unless doing so would frustrate the purpose of the statute.

principles.principle_label: Detention orders restrict constitutional and common law liberty
principles.paraphrase_en: Orders for detention under the Mental Health Ordinance restrict both constitutional rights under the Bill of Rights and common law rights to liberty, engaging requirements of procedural fairness.
The determination of whether section 68A of the Mental Health Ordinance applies to a particular class of persons is a question of statutory interpretation.

principles.principle_label: Conditional discharge order restricts personal liberty
principles.paraphrase_en: A conditional discharge order made under section 42B of the Mental Health Ordinance prima facie restricts a person's personal liberty, though such restriction may be justified by the proportionality test.

principles.principle_label: Purpose of mental health review tribunals
principles.paraphrase_en: Mental health review tribunals are designed to ensure that a patient's rights are practical and effective, and their procedures should be user-friendly to enable direct communication.

relationships.target_case_name: Ng Chun Kuen v HKSAR
relationships.description: The judgment applies the holding that a conditional discharge order under MHO s.42B prima facie restricts personal liberty.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Tze Yam v Commissioner of Police (No 2)
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that there may be a distinction between different types of liberty restrictions.

relationships.target_case_name: S, T, P v London Borough of Brent
relationships.description: The judgment cites this English case as an example of the common law supplementing statutory procedures to ensure fairness.

candidates.case_name: CHINA FORESTRY HOLDINGS CO LTD (in official liquidation) v. TOP WISDOM OVERSEAS HOLDINGS LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Ng J in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A fiduciary who receives a benefit in breach of their duty may hold that benefit on trust for their principal, and this principle is not necessarily confined only to cases involving bribes or secret commissions.

Where an agent or fiduciary makes an unauthorized profit by exploiting an opportunity arising from their position, equity may impose a constructive trust requiring them to account for that profit to their principal.
A proprietary claim based on an alleged constructive trust should not be struck out unless it is obviously unsustainable and has no real prospect of success.

relationships.target_case_name: FHR European Ventures LLP v Cedar Capital Partners LLC
relationships.description: The present judgment follows FHR's broader discussion of the equitable rule regarding proprietary remedies for fiduciary breaches, rejecting the argument that it is confined to bribes/secret commissions.

relationships.target_case_name: Sinclair Investments (UK) Ltd v Versailles Trade Finance Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment questions whether Sinclair remains fully sound authority, noting an arguable inconsistency between its formulation of the principle and that in FHR.

relationships.target_case_name: Carter v Palmer
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example where a fiduciary (a barrister) was held to have acquired discounted bills for his client, illustrating the application of the equitable rule.

relationships.target_case_name: Bowes v City of Toronto
relationships.description: Cited as a Privy Council authority where a mayor buying land for himself was held to be buying for the city, supporting the principle that unauthorized fiduciary profits are held for the principal.

candidates.case_name: TSUI KOON WAH v. IP MEI HO t/a MICHELLE IP & CO, SOLICITORS
judges: Before: Hon Marlene Ng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will only interfere with a lower court's costs order if the decision is wrong in principle or plainly wrong. The mere fact that the appellate court might have exercised the discretion differently is insufficient grounds for intervention.

principles.principle_label: Appellate interference with case management decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court should be slow to interfere with case management decisions, which are discretionary. The appellant faces a very high hurdle and must show the judge went clearly wrong, causing injustice, erred in principle, or made an irrational order.

An appellate court cannot interfere with a lower court's factual inference merely because other plausible inferences could be drawn from the material. Interference is only permitted if the decision exceeds the generous ambit of reasonable disagreement and is plainly wrong.

The distinction between nominal damages and small damages does not depend on the amount awarded. A token sum for nominal damages is different from a similarly small sum awarded as compensation for a trifling but proven loss.

principles.principle_label: Bailment liability and proof of loss
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant can establish liability for loss under the law of bailment by proving the bailee's breach of duty, but must also provide sufficient evidence to prove the quantum of the loss. Failure to sufficiently prove the value of the lost item limits the recoverable damages.

relationships.target_case_name: Ho Shu Kwong v Chiang Chun Yuan
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that an appellate court can only interfere with a costs order if it is wrong in principle or plainly wrong.

relationships.target_case_name: Kaifull Investments Ltd v Commissioner of Inland Revenue
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case on when an appellate court can interfere with a lower court's factual inferences.

relationships.target_case_name: Mimi Kar Kee Wong Hung v Severn Villa Ltd & ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles from this Court of Appeal case regarding the high threshold for appealing case management decisions.

relationships.target_case_name: In re Elgindata Ltd (No 2)
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principles governing the award of costs, noting they are applicable in the Small Claims Tribunal.

relationships.target_case_name: Roache v News Group Newspaper Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principles governing when an appellate court should intervene in a costs discretion.

relationships.target_case_name: Choy Yee Chun v Bond Star Development Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case alongside Roache for the principles on appellate intervention in costs discretion.

candidates.case_name: OGUZIE IKECHUKWU CHARLES v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP and Lisa Wong J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When assessing future risk of harm for non-refoulement claims, decision-makers must consider whether the feared ill-treatment originates from private disputes unrelated to protected grounds under refugee law, and whether the evidence supporting the risk is reliable rather than based on hearsay.
An appellate body may find an applicant untruthful based on inconsistent and changing accounts of material facts provided during hearings, which undermines the credibility of the claim.

principles.principle_label: Procedure for adducing new evidence on appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: New evidence cannot be adduced in support of appeal submissions without first obtaining leave from the court, and documents lodged without such leave will not be considered by the court.

If there has been a significant change of circumstances since a claim was finally determined, the proper procedure is for the applicant to make a subsequent claim rather than attempting to introduce new evidence on appeal without leave.

relationships.target_case_name: 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees
relationships.description: The judgment applies the non-refoulement principle under Article 33 of the Convention in assessing the risk of persecution.

candidates.case_name: ARMAN AZAM v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Before: Hon G Lam and Chow JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for non-refoulement decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: The court will not interfere with a Torture Claims Appeal Board decision unless it identifies an error of law, procedural unfairness, or findings that are Wednesbury unreasonable or irrational.

Non-refoulement claims are assessed with reference to the principle under Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees and its 1967 Protocol, which prohibits returning a person to a country where they would face persecution.

relationships.target_case_name: Re: Kartini
relationships.description: The judgment references the Court of Appeal's decision in Re: Kartini as relevant authority on non-refoulement matters.

candidates.case_name: KHATUN MST MUKTI v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of non-refoulement under Article 33 of the 1951 Refugee Convention and its 1967 Protocol prohibits the return of a person to a territory where they would face a risk of persecution.

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for factual findings
principles.paraphrase_en: A court will not interfere with the factual findings of an administrative board unless those findings are affected by an error of law, procedural unfairness, or are Wednesbury unreasonable/irrational.

relationships.target_case_name: Re: Kartini
relationships.description: The present judgment cites the Court of Appeal's decision in Re: Kartini as relevant authority.

candidates.case_name: SHAH SABIR HUSSAIN v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of non-refoulement under Article 33 of the 1951 Refugee Convention and its 1967 Protocol prohibits returning a person to a territory where they would face a risk of persecution.
A court will not interfere with an administrative board's decision unless it finds an error of law, procedural unfairness, or that the decision is Wednesbury unreasonable or irrational.

relationships.target_case_name: Re: Kartini
relationships.description: The present judgment references the Court of Appeal's decision in Re: Kartini as relevant authority on the applicable legal principles.

candidates.case_name: RE VICTOR VITUS CHIZOBA
judges: Before: Hon Cheung and Chu JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof in non-refoulement claims
principles.paraphrase_en: The applicant bears the burden of establishing a well-founded fear of persecution or a real risk of torture or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment to succeed in a non-refoulement claim.

The decision-maker (such as the Torture Claims Appeal Board) must assess the credibility of the applicant's evidence, including its consistency and corroboration by country of origin information, and may reject the claim in its entirety if the applicant is found not to be a witness of truth.

The standard for a successful non-refoulement claim is the existence of a 'real risk' that the applicant will face persecution, torture, or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment if returned.

The principle of non-refoulement, which prohibits returning a person to a country where they would face persecution, is derived from Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees and its 1967 Protocol.

candidates.case_name: HENZULEA MARIAN NICOLAE v. YAO LIQIONG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Damian Wong in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust can, in exceptional circumstances, be established after the property in question has been acquired, not only at the time of acquisition.

relationships.target_case_name: Primecredit
relationships.description: The judgment cites the reasoning of Cheung JA in Primecredit to support the principle that a common intention constructive trust can arise post-acquisition.

candidates.case_name: TO KIN WAH AND OTHERS v. TO KAM CHAU AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Court

principles.principle_label: Application of Chinese custom to t'ong management
principles.paraphrase_en: The appointment of managers for a t'ong (a Chinese customary trust) is governed by Chinese law and custom, including the specific customs of the clan and the t'ong itself.

principles.principle_label: Equal representation principle in clan management
principles.paraphrase_en: Under the custom of the clan and t'ong, each of the major family branches (Fongs) should have an equal number of managers and equal say in important matters affecting the clan and t'ong.
Under local custom, each major family branch (Fong) should have full autonomy in managing its own internal affairs without interference from other Fongs.

candidates.case_name: HIP HING CONSTRUCTION COMPANY LTD v. HONG KONG AIRLINES LTD
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Court

principles.principle_label: Trust creation without segregation for fungible assets
principles.paraphrase_en: A valid trust can be created over intangible, fungible assets (like money or identical shares) without requiring physical segregation or appropriation of specific units, provided the subject matter is sufficiently certain in quantity and the intention to create a trust is clear.

principles.principle_label: Certainty of subject matter for fungibles
principles.paraphrase_en: The requirement for certainty of subject matter in a trust is satisfied for fungible assets if the trust property forms a defined part of a homogeneous mass, and the beneficiary's proportionate share of that mass can be precisely identified from records, even if individual units are not earmarked.

principles.principle_label: Contractual retention money as trust property
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a construction contract clause expressly provides that retention money is to be held by the employer 'upon trust' for the contractor, this can create a valid trust over those funds for the contractor's benefit, subject to the employer's contractual rights of recourse.

principles.principle_label: Trust assets excluded from insolvency distribution
principles.paraphrase_en: If money is held by a company on trust for another party, that money does not form part of the company's general assets available for distribution to its creditors under a statutory compromise or liquidation scheme.

relationships.target_case_name: Re CA Pacific Finance Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from CA Pacific that certainty of subject matter for a trust over fungible securities is established through detailed records identifying the mass held for beneficiaries, even without physical segregation.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Kayford Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites Kayford as an example concerning the creation of a trust over customer prepayments for goods, involving the use of a separate bank account.

relationships.target_case_name: Rayack Construction Ltd v Lampeter Meat Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites Rayack for the proposition that a construction contract clause can impose an obligation on an employer to set aside retention money as a separate trust fund.

relationships.target_case_name: Hunter v Moss
relationships.description: The judgment cites Hunter v Moss for the principle that a trust over a specified number of identical shares in a larger homogeneous holding is sufficiently certain without identifying the specific shares.

candidates.case_name: CHAN WING YAN AND ANOTHER v. JP-EX CRYPTO ASSET PLATFORM PTY LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge G. Chow in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Cryptocurrencies, including USDT (Tether), constitute property capable of forming the subject matter of a trust.
To establish an express trust, three certainties must be present: certainty of subject matter, certainty of object, and certainty of intention.

principles.principle_label: Trust over fungible mass without specific appropriation
principles.paraphrase_en: A trust over part of a fungible mass (like cryptocurrency holdings) does not fail for uncertainty of subject matter if the beneficiary's proportionate share is ascertainable, even without appropriation of specific assets.

principles.principle_label: Role of crypto exchange as bare trustee
principles.paraphrase_en: A cryptocurrency exchange platform, when holding users' assets, can act as a bare trustee, holding assets on trust for account holders and following their instructions regarding beneficial interests.
A Quistclose trust arises when property is transferred with the objective intention that it be used only for a specific purpose, imposing a trust on the funds for that purpose.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Gatecoin Ltd (in Liq)
relationships.description: The judgment follows Re Gatecoin's approach of applying the UK Jurisdiction Task Force's legal statement on cryptoassets and its analysis of express trust requirements.

relationships.target_case_name: Feng Bo
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Feng Bo that USDT is property capable of being held on trust.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Lehman Brothers Intl (Europe)
relationships.description: The judgment cites Re Lehman Brothers for the principle that a trust over a proportionate share of a fungible mass does not fail for uncertainty.

relationships.target_case_name: Ruscoe v Cryptopia
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from Ruscoe v Cryptopia regarding the role of a cryptocurrency exchange as a bare trustee for account holders.

relationships.target_case_name: Barclays Bank Ltd v Quistclose Investments Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites Quistclose as the origin of the principle regarding trusts arising from property transferred for a specific purpose.

relationships.target_case_name: China Life Trustees Limited v China Energy Reserve and Chemicals Group Overseas
relationships.description: The judgment cites this CFA decision for its explanation of how a Quistclose trust arises.

candidates.case_name: HIP HING CONSTRUCTION COMPANY LTD v. HONG KONG AIRLINES LTD
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The test for granting an interlocutory injunction is whether there is a serious question to be tried on the plaintiff's claim and whether, on a balance of probabilities, the court should grant the interim orders sought.

A contractual clause stating that retention money 'shall be held upon trust' by the employer for the contractor is sufficient to create a trust, and this trust coexists with any provision allowing the employer to deduct sums by way of set-off.

principles.principle_label: Certainty of subject matter in trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A trust created by a retention clause is not invalid for lack of certainty of subject matter, even where the employer has a right of deduction or set-off against the fund.
Where a retention clause creates a trust, the contractor/beneficiary is entitled to seek a mandatory order requiring the employer to set aside the retention money in a separate trust fund.
Granting an interim mandatory injunction to set up a trust fund for retention monies forestalls the risk of injustice to the contractor if the employer becomes insolvent.

relationships.target_case_name: American Cyanamid Co v Ethicon
relationships.description: The judgment applies the test from this case for granting an interlocutory injunction.

relationships.target_case_name: Concorde Construction Co Ltd v Colgan Co Ltd (No 1)
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle from this case that an injunction should be granted to compel an employer to set up a trust fund for retention monies.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Hsin Chong Construction Co Ltd (Provisional Liquidators: Application for Directions)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Court of Appeal's analysis of the exact retention clause and its conclusion that a set-off provision does not affect the trust created.

relationships.target_case_name: Yew Sang Hong Ltd v Hong Kong Housing Authority
relationships.description: The judgment notes that Re Hsin Chong addressed a contrary view from this case regarding the effect of a set-off provision on the existence of a trust.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Tout and Finch Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the authority that specific words in a contract are sufficient to create a trust over retention monies.

relationships.target_case_name: Rayack Construction Ltd v Lampeter Meat Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that an employer, as fiduciary of retention monies, can be ordered to set them aside in a separate account.

candidates.case_name: NORMAN GUY DONALD AND ANOTHER v. LEE MICHAEL CHAK CHUEN in his capacity as executor of the estate of LI PAK IU AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Chu VP, Yuen and Au JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Interpretation of void gifts in wills
principles.paraphrase_en: When a gift in a will clause is void from the beginning (void ab initio), there is no property created that trustees can subsequently apply for the benefit of other potential beneficiaries under the same clause.

principles.principle_label: Application of contractual severability to wills
principles.paraphrase_en: The doctrine of severability, which is a principle of contract law, does not automatically apply to the construction of wills.

principles.principle_label: Test for invalidity affecting other objects in a will clause
principles.paraphrase_en: The test from Re Canning's Will Trusts may be applied to determine whether the invalidity of one gift or object in a will clause invalidates another object, such as a trustee's power, within the same clause.

principles.principle_label: Primary of clear language in will interpretation
principles.paraphrase_en: Where the language of a will clause is clear, extrinsic considerations such as the adequacy of provisions for other family members become of little materiality in interpretation.

relationships.target_case_name: Midland Business Management Ltd v Lo Man Kui
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes the application of the contractual severability doctrine from this case to the construction of wills.

relationships.target_case_name: Sadler v Imperial Life Assurance Co of Canada Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes the contractual principle of severability applied in this case from the principles governing will construction.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Canning's Will Trusts
relationships.description: The judgment applies the test from this case to determine the effect of one void gift on other objects within the same will clause.

candidates.case_name: BLUESUN CAPITAL LTD v. TEWOO GROUP FINANCE NO 3 LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Mr Recorder Manzoni, SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: For the purpose of determining whether a bondholder meets a specified percentage threshold to direct trustee action, bonds that are beneficially held by or on behalf of the issuer, guarantor, or their subsidiaries are deemed not to be outstanding, even if not formally cancelled.

Whether bonds are beneficially held for an issuer is a question of fact to be determined by reference to the intention of the acquiring entity and the issuer at the time of acquisition. The party asserting beneficial ownership bears the burden of establishing that intention.

A trustee holds money received for bond payments on trust for application according to the trust deed. A bondholder meeting a specified percentage threshold of outstanding bonds is entitled to require the trustee to bring an action for recovery.

candidates.case_name: KWONG YI LING (suing on behalf of herself and all other shareholders in the 6th Defendant except the 1st and 2nd Defendants) v. LAU KWUN LEUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Linda Chan J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Scope of assistance in breach of trust
principles.paraphrase_en: The concept of 'assistance' in a breach of trust is not limited to acts that occur before or at the same time as the breach. The nature of the breach determines what constitutes assistance.

relationships.description: The judgment references the textbook 'Lewin on Trust' for an explanation of the principle concerning assistance in a breach of trust.
The judgment references the textbook 'Bullen & Leake' for an alternative statement of the principle concerning assistance in a breach of trust.

candidates.case_name: CHINA MEDICAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC (IN LIQUIDATION) AND OTHERS v. SAMSON TSANG TAK YUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Au and Chow JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Stable-door point as part of risk assessment
principles.paraphrase_en: The 'stable-door' point concerning whether assets have already been dissipated is not a separate legal issue but forms part of the overall assessment of the risk of dissipation of assets when considering an injunction.

principles.principle_label: Equity will not act in vain
principles.paraphrase_en: The principle that equity will not act in vain, meaning an injunction will not be granted if the defendant's assets have already been dissipated, is reflected in the consideration of the 'stable-door' point.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Chau Cham Wong Patrick
relationships.description: The judgment references this case as having cited the principle that equity will not act in vain in the context of the 'stable-door' point.

relationships.target_case_name: Convoy
relationships.description: The judgment notes that the principle from Re Chau Cham Wong Patrick was cited with approval in this case.

relationships.target_case_name: Ras al Khaimah Investment Authority v Bestfort Development LLP
relationships.description: The judgment notes that the English Court of Appeal in this case criticized and rejected the 'stable-door' point principle.

candidates.case_name: CHINACHEM CHARITABLE FOUNDATION LTD v. CHAN WAI TONG CHRISTOPHER AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung JA and Yuen JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A claim alleging fraud or dishonest conduct must be struck out if it is advanced without any proper factual basis to support the allegation.

A court may strike out a statement of claim on grounds including that it discloses no reasonable cause of action, is scandalous, frivolous or vexatious, may prejudice or delay a fair trial, or is an abuse of court process.
On appeal against a striking out order, the appellate court will consider whether the matters relied on by the claimant raise any triable issues, even making assumptions in the claimant's favour.

candidates.case_name: JSP INTERNATIONAL S.R.O. v. ALACRITY LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Cheng J in Chambers (by Paper Disposal)

principles.paraphrase_en: An allegation of fraud or dishonesty must be pleaded with sufficient particularity. It is insufficient to merely state facts that could be consistent with honest conduct; the specific primary facts from which dishonesty is to be inferred must be clearly set out so the defendant knows the case to meet.

A court may decline to grant a declaration that a defendant holds funds on constructive trust, even where it is prepared to declare that the plaintiff has a proprietary interest in or ownership of those funds. The two forms of relief are distinct.

relationships.target_case_name: Edison Norge AS
relationships.description: The present judgment cites Edison Norge AS as an example where declarations of proprietary interest were granted but declarations of a constructive trust were refused.

candidates.case_name: EVERCARE HEALTH LTD v. SO LAI KAN also known as GOBBY SO AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge B. Mak in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A proprietary injunction must be connected to a specific asset, or its traceable proceeds, that is held by or under the control of the defendant and is the subject of a proprietary claim by the plaintiff.

To obtain a proprietary injunction, the plaintiff must generally provide reasonable evidence showing that the specific asset (or its traceable proceeds) exists and is held by or under the control of the defendant.

principles.principle_label: Limitation on injunction for dissipated assets
principles.paraphrase_en: If the asset that is the subject of the proprietary claim has been dissipated and is no longer traceable, a proprietary injunction will not normally be available.

candidates.case_name: CHINA MEDICAL TECHNOLOGIES INC (In Liquidation) AND OTHERS v. THE BANK OF EAST ASIA, LTD
judges: Before: Hon Ng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: An allegation of fraud or dishonesty must be pleaded with sufficient particularity, including the primary facts from which dishonesty is to be inferred; particulars that are equally consistent with honesty are insufficient.

principles.principle_label: Bank's receipt of funds - ministerial vs beneficial capacity
principles.paraphrase_en: It is not a settled principle of law that a bank always receives funds in a purely ministerial capacity when a trustee deposits trust money into a personal account; the bank may potentially receive it beneficially, making it a complex and unsettled area unsuitable for striking out.

principles.principle_label: Fraud not essential for knowing receipt or want of authority
principles.paraphrase_en: Fraud is not an essential element of claims based on knowing receipt or want of authority, meaning a statutory limitation period applicable to actions based on fraud does not necessarily apply to such claims.

The practice of deciding preliminary points of law should generally be confined to cases where the facts are complicated and the legal issue is short and easily decided; otherwise, it may increase cost and time without true procedural economy.

principles.principle_label: Fraudulent trading vs dishonest assistance distinction
principles.paraphrase_en: Liability for fraudulent trading under statute requires that the defendant carried on the business of the company in a fraudulent manner; this is distinct from, and should not be equated with, liability for dishonestly assisting breaches of duty by directors.

relationships.target_case_name: Hing Yip Holdings (Hong Kong) Limited v Cellmark China Limited
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for its obiter analysis that fraud is not an essential element of claims based on want of authority and knowing receipt.

relationships.target_case_name: Beaman v A.R.T.S. Limited
relationships.description: The judgment references this case in the context of discussing when a statutory limitation period for fraud-based actions applies.

relationships.target_case_name: George Chu v Tan Giong Seng Johnson
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that it is not in the interests of justice to relieve a party from an unless order if they consciously decided not to comply.

candidates.case_name: TANG GANG AND OTHERS v. WONG SAI CHUNG
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge H. Au-Yeung (Paper Disposal)

principles.paraphrase_en: A final and conclusive foreign judgment will be enforced in Hong Kong unless the foreign proceedings violated fundamental principles of justice or enforcement would be contrary to Hong Kong public policy.
The public policy exception to enforcing a foreign judgment is narrow and must be applied restrictively.
To establish fraud as a ground for challenging a foreign judgment, the evidence must meet a standard commensurate with the gravity of the allegation.

Where fraud allegations have been determined abroad, summary judgment or striking out is appropriate in subsequent proceedings unless there is plausible evidence showing at least a prima facie case of fraud.

candidates.case_name: NAEEM MUHAMMAD v. HONG KONG SAR GOVERNMENT
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining whether a period of immigration detention has become or will become unreasonable and thus unlawful, the weight assigned to specific factors may change over an extended period of detention.

The principles governing the lawfulness of immigration detention, as summarized in relevant Court of Appeal authority, should be adopted and applied as appropriate to the specific circumstances of the individual case.

relationships.target_case_name: Harjang Singh v Secretary for Security
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principles summarized by the Court of Appeal in Harjang Singh for assessing the lawfulness of immigration detention.

candidates.case_name: HUI CHUN PING v. HUI KAU MO
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Lord Hoffmann NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: The limitation rule that time does not run against a trustee in possession of trust property applies only to express or consensual trusts (category 1), not to constructive trusts arising from fraud or breach of duty (category 2).

The fiction that a fiduciary holds property acquired in breach of duty on constructive trust serves the specific purpose of preventing the fiduciary from profiting from the property, not for general limitation purposes.
Legal fictions and deeming provisions are created for particular purposes and should not be automatically extended to other legal contexts without justification.

principles.principle_label: No discretion in constructive trust imposition
principles.paraphrase_en: The imposition of a constructive trust on a fiduciary who acquires property through breach of duty is not a matter of judicial discretion but follows settled legal principles applied to the facts.

relationships.target_case_name: Williams v. Central Bank of Nigeria
relationships.description: The judgment follows the majority Supreme Court decision rejecting the argument that limitation periods apply equally to consensual and constructive trusts.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v. Hon Kam Wing
relationships.description: The judgment references this Hong Kong case where a bribe recipient was treated as a category 1 trustee for limitation purposes.

relationships.target_case_name: Attorney-General for Hong Kong v. Reid
relationships.description: The judgment discusses this Privy Council decision that established an employer's proprietary interest in bribes received by an employee.

relationships.target_case_name: Gwembe Valley Development Co Ltd v. Koshy (No 3)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the distinction from this English case between category 1 and category 2 trustees for limitation purposes.

relationships.target_case_name: Metropolitan Bank v. Heiron
relationships.description: The judgment expresses doubt about dicta suggesting judicial discretion in imposing constructive trusts, stating they were wrong.

candidates.case_name: LI XIUHUA v. FT ELECTRONIC CO LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Suen SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant may potentially establish a defense of ministerial receipt by demonstrating they received funds as an agent of another party, holding those funds on trust for that principal, which can negate a proprietary claim against them.

Where traceable proceeds are paid into a bank account operated as a running account with frequent withdrawals and deposits, and where there are competing equitable interests from innocent contributors, the first-in-first-out rule from Clayton's Case may apply instead of the principle of subordination to determine attribution of payments out.

relationships.target_case_name: Clayton's Case
relationships.description: The judgment references the first-in-first-out rule or presumption established in Clayton's Case for attributing payments out of a running bank account with mixed funds.

candidates.case_name: REMX LIMITED Plaintiff and LAE TECHNOLOGIES HONG KONG LIMiteD 1st Defendant LEGACY TRUST COMPANY LIMITED 2nd Defendant
judges: Before: Master Alexander Tang in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A person who receives assets as a trustee can still be subject to a claim for unjust enrichment; the status as trustee is not a complete bar to such a claim.

principles.principle_label: Constructive trust upon notice of termination
principles.paraphrase_en: When a party holding funds receives notice that the relationship between the payer and the intended recipient has been terminated and that the funds are to be recalled, that party holds the funds on trust for the payer and has no right to transfer them to the original intended recipient.

principles.principle_label: Quistclose trust upon failure of purpose
principles.paraphrase_en: Where money is transferred for a specific purpose and that purpose fails, the recipient holds the money on a resulting trust (Quistclose trust) for the transferor.

principles.principle_label: Agent's duty regarding funds subject to equitable interest
principles.paraphrase_en: An agent who becomes aware that funds cannot be used for their designated purpose and are subject to a trust claim or equitable interest of another party cannot deal with those funds in a manner inconsistent with that interest.

In determining the defendant's enrichment for a restitution claim, the court must consider not just the amount received but also any liabilities the defendant incurred as a consequence of receiving the money.

relationships.target_case_name: Skandinaviska Enskilda Banken AB (Publ) v Conway
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Privy Council authority for the principle that a trustee can be sued for unjust enrichment and for the approach to measuring enrichment.

relationships.target_case_name: Jeremy Stone Consultants Ltd v. National Westminster Bank plc
relationships.description: The judgment cites this English High Court case as judicial authority recognizing defenses like good faith change of position and ministerial receipt in unjust enrichment claims.
